body_txt: An Ordinary High-School Student is shown associating with a small, presumably tight-knit circle of friends. But of course, the Call to Adventure arrives, and our intrepid hero is plucked from normalcy and told that he's The Chosen One . Cool. A couple training montages and plot twists later, the hero finds himself in a tight spot and wanting for allies. Now would be a great time to call upon those friends that trust and like him enough to believe that the Statue of Liberty is actually a giant evil robot . Too bad those friends have ceased to exist within the plot. They are Social Circle Filler. These characters existed to avoid presenting the protagonist as a friendless loner , before falling victim to Conservation of Detail . Despite the appearance of close friendship with the hero, they lose relevance after he's drawn into The Masquerade and introduced to new friends that aren't Locked Out of the Loop . There is some Truth in Television in that being friends with your classmates is often due to a lack of choice, as you're forced to spend your time in school with them so you might as well get along. Many people also are on friendly terms with their classmates or coworkers without wanting them in their personal lives. Notably, fiction often ignores the fact that kids and teenagers can have close friends who don't go the same school as them. Contrast Friendless Background . Compare Dropped After the Pilot , where character who seem to be core cast members are quickly dropped; Forgotten Fallen Friend , where the death of a friend is used to set off the plot before being forgotten; and 24-Hour Party People , where hitherto-unseen "friends" appear to make a party more lively. Don't confuse with Those Two Guys , as they have a higher chance of coming back despite occasionally filling the same niche. See also The Friends Who Never Hang , where specific characters in a friend group rarely interact.
